reason: The machine you operate across the network is the remote computer; the one in front of you is local.
WYSIWYG = What You See Is What You Get.
SMTP is the mail-sending protocol; POP3 retrieves, FTP transfers files, Outlook Express is a client not a protocol.
Telephone lines use the 6-position RJ-11 jack; RJ-45 is 8-position Ethernet.
At reset the CPU fetches from the reset vector, which maps to firmware in ROM BIOS; boot.ini/CONFIG.SYS are loaded far later.
Incorrect CMOS drive geometry/type settings produce the classic 'fixed disk error' POST message.
Open slots break the intended case airflow path, causing overheating (EMI is a defensible second answer but overheating is the standard one).
PCI cards are jumperless; IRQ assignment/availability is shown by the motherboard BIOS setup (PnP/PCI configuration).
10/100 denotes 10 Mbps / 100 Mbps Ethernet link speeds.
ATX uses a single 20-pin keyed power connector; AT/Baby AT use the split P8/P9 pair.
Tracks are divided into sectors; clusters are a filesystem grouping of sectors.
An ESD wrist strap contains a ~1 megohm current-limiting resistor for operator safety.
IEEE 1284 is the parallel-port standard; RS-232 is serial and CAT 5 is cabling.
Conditioning = primary corona/charge roller applies a uniform (negative) charge to the photosensitive drum.
Smudges/grime on keycaps are removed with a mild all-purpose cleaner; denatured alcohol is reserved for contacts, and solvents/silicone can damage plastic.
ESD damages low-voltage CMOS logic on expansion boards; power supplies, monitors and keyboards are comparatively rugged.
The DOS MEM command (MEM /C) lists programs resident in memory, i.e. TSRs; MEMMAKER optimizes rather than lists.
Of the listed bus types, PCI is the modern/highest-performance one with forward compatibility; ISA/EISA are legacy.
Standard service practice: swap FRUs one at a time in the recommended order so the actual failure is identified, and return unused parts to stock.
UNIVAC = UNIVersal Automatic Computer (Eckert-Mauchly, 1951).
